I'm not sure that this is the right thing to do. Per [PEP 358](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0358/), Python2 strings are overloaded to hold both characters and bytes. In Python3, strings are sequences of characters only and there is a separate type for sequences of bytes. Since a TOML file must contain only UTF-8 encoded characters, I think accepting a sequence of bytes may not be the right thing to do.

True fair scheduling turns out to be super simple to implement -- you basically just need to keep a counter on each task; every time you run a task you increment the counter by how long it ran for, and when picking a task to run then run the one with the smallest counter value. There are a few details I'm skipping over and the theory of *why* this works turns out to be much more complicated than you'd expect (and I'm too lazy to type it all here :-)), but the actual implementation is extremely simple and pretty cheap.

It's not clear how much of a win it actually is in practice; probably its benefits are diluted both by the use of cooperative instead of pre-emptive scheduling, and by most curio programs being I/O bound rather than CPU bound. But in theory it seems like it could help servers provide better responsivity under load, e.g. like this question that glyph pointed to: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40601119/python-ssh-server-twisted-conch-takes-up-high-cpu-usage-when-a-large-number-of/40627446

Recursive upload with a few fixes: a bit faster, safer path handling

```python
import posixpath
import os
import yadisk

def recursive_upload(y, from_dir, to_dir):
     for root, dirs, files in os.walk(from_dir):
         p = root.split(from_dir)[1].strip(os.path.sep)
         dir_path = posixpath.join(to_dir, p)
         
         try:
             y.mkdir(dir_path)
         except yadisk.exceptions.PathExistsError:
             pass
 
         for file in files:
             file_path = posixpath.join(dir_path, file)
             p_sys = p.replace("/", os.path.sep)
             in_path = os.path.join(from_dir, p_sys, file)
             try:
                 y.upload(in_path, file_path)
             except yadisk.exceptions.PathExistsError:
                 pass

y = yadisk.YaDisk(token="<токен>")
to_dir = "/test"
from_dir = "/home/ubuntu"

recursive_upload(y, from_dir, to_dir)
```

@Make42, thank you for making me aware of dfply, I did not know about it. I have chosen to reply here instead of at has2k1/plydata#3 since I can do a little more than just acknowledge learning about dfply.

I knew about dplython when I created plydata, in fact I had thought about it long before and had a mock [implementation](https://github.com/dodger487/dplython/issues/6#issuecomment-205608816) with which I tried to [influence](https://github.com/dodger487/dplython/issues/22#issuecomment-207261809) the direction of dplython. I did not work and I could not adapt to dplython.

Specifically, I did not like the conversion to a special dataframe and I felt that the manager variable `X` was clunky. A string evaluation based implementation helps get around both issues.

Nonetheless, it does pain me to see a duplication of efforts in the open-source world. Concerning dfply and plydata, the efforts will probably go on since both are mature enough and have distinct design choices. That is, dfply goes for near syntax compatibility with dplyr e.g.

```python
>> select(starts_with('c'))
```

Whereas plydata (`>>` notwithstanding) tries to be more "pythonic" e.g.

```python
>> select(startswith='c')
```

Reason for the difference is, in pytthon `str.startswith` does not have an underscore and using a keyword argument reduces the number of variables in the global namespace. 

Anyhow, if I had seen this project get off the ground I would have tried (with more zeal this time) to infect @kieferk with string evaluation ideas.

I don't think I ever said that the changes to Flask-SQLAlchemy would make alchy unnecessary. I was referring to Flask-Alchy extension which was was primarily developed to take advantage of the work done in Flask-SQLAlchemy around session management within the Flask request/response cycle.

Alchy is a standalone ORM-session layer that extends functionality provided by SQLAlchemy without any dependence on a web framework. It was inspired by Flask-SQLAlchemy but has since gone beyond what Flask-SQLAlchemy provides.

_ASIDE: I've pretty much stopped development of alchy in favor of its successor, [sqlservice](https://github.com/dgilland/sqlservice), which has taken a slightly different design approach while still trying to achieve some level of feature parity with alchy._

At the time when Flask-Alchy was created, there wasn't an easy way to provide your own declarative base model for use within the Flask-SQLAlchemy extension. You had to basically subclass Flask-SQLAlchemy and override where necessary.

The blog post that I wrote was in response to developers I worked with and questions I had seen on forums asking how to use Flask-SQLAlchemy without Flask. There seemed to be some misunderstanding/confusing about Flask-SQLAlchemy's role and how to extract some of its functionality out of the Flask specific items. While I think Flask-SQLAlchemy is great, I think it would have worked better as two libraries (one for improving upon SQLAlchemy and another as a much thinner integration layer between a SQLAlchemy session manager and Flask). Too many good features made their way into Flask-SQLAlchemy but then required that they could only be used with a Flask application context.

So I would say that once the custom model and query class features in Flask-SQLAlchemy are implemented, then the need for Flask-Alchy would no longer exist.

`_` is an instance of the underscore class.
Retrieving any attribute results in an [attribute getter](https://docs.python.org/2/library/operator.html#operator.attrgetter) for such an attribute.
Calling it invokes this attribute getter with the `'tif'` string as its first argument.
The endswith attribute of str however is a callable method.
You then call it on another string. `tif` doesn't end in `image.tif` so the result is false.
What happens is `attrgetter('endswith')('tif')('image.tif')` which is more like `'tif'.endswith('image.tif')`

Yeah, adding support for record sets to Route53 (and a couple other providers) is still necessary. 

From what I've seen regarding LetEncrypt clients (like dehydrated, Certbot, Acme.sh) they are usually pretty good about cleaning up after themselves on success (by calling `lexicon delete`). 

Once we get the remaining lexicon providers updated to support record sets, this will be almost a non-issue, as failed ACME challenge dns records will not stop lexicon from creating new ACME challenge records.

However we'll still have a couple of providers that do not support record sets. They will have to be handled differently. 

In general I favor `explicit` changes over `implicit` changes. I'm also a fan of `internal consistency` between providers.

I'd rather have a low level tool like lexicon do exactly what I tell it (and fail quickly if it cannot do what I want) instead of automatically deleting & recreating records in some cases but not others. 

Just my thoughts, but yeah, getting the remaining providers working with record sets a priority right now. I think I'll create a github project for it, to track them all.

### Type checks
Those TypeErrors will be raised organically though, as the the objects are iterated and called.  Builtins (like `sorted`) don't go out of their way to pre-check them.
```
In []: sorted([], key=0)
Out[]: []
```

### Performance
Yes, the smaller the buckets, the less the optimization.  But still the baseline is a relatively slow loop of O(V), whereas the additional copy is a fast inlined loop of O(K), where K <= V.  So it's not the size of the `dict` per se, but rather how little bucket-ing occurs.

One under-utilized feature of `defaultdict` is that if the `default_factory` is set to `None`, it behaves as a `dict` again.  That would be the fastest option of all.  Some comparison timings for buckets of size 2:
```
In []: %timeit bucketize(range(10 ** 6), (2).__rfloordiv__)
1 loop, best of 3: 428 ms per loop

In []: %timeit bucketize_default(range(10 ** 6), (2).__rfloordiv__)
1 loop, best of 3: 412 ms per loop

In []: %timeit bucketize_default_copy(range(10 ** 6), (2).__rfloordiv__)
1 loop, best of 3: 441 ms per loop
```

```py
#!python3
import keyboard
import threading

typing = False

    global typing
    print("Are programly typed: " + str(typing) + ", key: "+ kbd_event.name + ", event: " + kbd_event.event_type)

def endedTyping(sleep_time):
    global typing
    global etThread
    time.sleep(sleep_time)
    typing = False

def hkAction(to_type):
    global typing
    global etThread
    if not typing: # Prevent call while working(typing)
        typing = True
        sleep_time = len(to_type) * 0.01 # Calculate time to sleep, 10 ms for every character
        print("Starting to typing...")
        keyboard.write(to_type)
        if not etThread.is_alive(): # Prevent already started thread exception
            etThread = threading.Thread(target=endedTyping, args=(sleep_time,))
            etThread.start()

keyboard.hook(HookCallback)
keyboard.register_hotkey('f7', lambda: hkAction("hello world."))
keyboard.wait()
```

> If we were to wrap around sklearn it would involve mimicking the mir_eval output structure etc., and I wonder whether that's in/outside the scope of jams.

I think it's outside the scope of jams.

FWIW, our previous solution in #20 was to copy-paste the relevant bits of code into mir_eval.  Hurray for BSD licensing! (Also, the install problems referenced in that thread should no longer be a problem since all the C dependencies have moved over to shipping wheels.)

I'm warming a bit to the idea of mir_eval including tagging and retrieval metrics, but it will take some serious thought.

- What's the scope?  Within-track annotations (eg time-varying tagging) or across-track (eg cover retrieval)?  The current mir_eval conventions are really geared toward comparing annotations on a track-by-track basis, so it makes sense to start there.
- What does the output format look like?  In general, we might have multi-label data and want scores on a per-label basis.  In that case, the output dictionary from `evaluate` might have an unspecified key set, or at least a level of indirection, eg, `{'F1_micro': {'guitar': 0.7, 'bass': 0.3, ...}, 'Precision': {...}}`.
- What does the input format look like?  I'm a a fan of `(interval, label)` tuples (maybe with a confidence/score attached) but that might be insufficient for certain applications.  I think it makes more sense to do it this way and then parameterize the metrics by sampling rate (eg 10hz as in segmentation), rather than accept point-wise annotations, which makes comparison between annotations at different frame rates more difficult.
- What would an api look like for corpus-level evaluation, like cover retrieval or full-track tagging?  Would we even need to define such a thing, or just expose the ranking and classification metrics in such a way as to make it easy?

re: double octave, how do we feel about introducing an argument that determines the length of `semitone_bitmap`? this could be specified as an integer (12, 24, etc), which would allow arbitrary length semitone encodings ... I'm currently unsure of the value of this flexibility, but I could see it being handy. Alternatively, it could be a binary flag to indicate single / double octaves?

re: default value for pitch quality reduction ... these additional scale degrees need to be ignored for most scoring algorithms (maj/min, thirds, sevenths, etc), because the bit-vector represents _semitones_, and **not** pitch classes. To illustrate, `sevenths(C:9, C:7) = 1`, with the non-zero semitones being `[0, 4, 7, 10, 14]` vs `[0, 4, 7, 10]`, and are equal when the 14 is ignored; but `sevenths(C:7(2), C:7) = 0`, with the non-zero semitones being `[0, 2, 4, 7, 10]` vs `[0, 4, 7, 10]`, because the D now conflicts in the comparison. 

IIRC, the only metric that looks at pitch class intersection is MIREX, in which case a quality bitmap is required, and a double-octave doesn't make much sense (the upper half could be empty?).

    from django.conf import settings

What @Gadgetoid said. Even if you can bring Pillow to MicroPython, you're going to encounter a lot of restrictions. I recently had to make a small 8266-powered device - a 16x2 display library, a RC522 library and lots of code in `main.py`. Well, I ended up having to set up a MP toolchain on my laptop and compile both libraries into `.mpy` (or `.mpc` ? ) files, so that they wouldn't throw the device into an `OutOfMemory` error while importing both modules - since MP compiles each cleartext (`.py`) module while importing it, it crashes if there's not enough memory to do so. I also remember sorting out some inheritance fuckery last time I brought my pure Python modules to MP - IIRC attributes of parent class wouldn't be accessible for some reason when introspecting in MP console. That said, it's still an amazing and empowering tool, and I'd pay money to continue using it whenever I feel like it, but you need to be careful when using it. I hope the extended RAM of ESP32 will solve some of these problems.

I don't think filter_nodes is released yet on pypy so I switched to use filter.

This example should work for python 2 now.

```python
from treelib import Node, Tree
from pprint import pprint
import json

tree = Tree()
tree.create_node("Harry", "harry")  # root node
tree.create_node("Jane", "jane", parent="harry")
tree.create_node("Bill", "bill", parent="harry")
tree.create_node("Diane", "diane", parent="jane")
tree.create_node("Mary", "mary", parent="diane")
tree.create_node("Mark", "mark", parent="jane")


def sort_tree(tree_dict, key_value_function, reverse=None):

  def child_key_value(child_tag):
    if isinstance(child_tag, dict): child_tag = next(iter(child_tag.keys()))
    return key_value_function(tree.filter_nodes(lambda node: node.tag == child_tag)[0])
    return key_value_function(filter(lambda node: node.tag == child_tag, tree.nodes)[0])

  if isinstance(tree_dict, dict):
    tree_dict[next(iter(tree_dict.keys()))]['children'].sort(key=child_key_value, reverse=reverse)
    for child in tree_dict[tree_dict.keys()[0]]['children']:
      sort_tree(key_value_function, key_value_function)

  return tree_dict


pprint(tree.to_dict())
print()
print(json.dumps(sort_tree(tree.to_dict(), lambda node: node.tag, True)))
```

I've changed this to now write to a file not use sqlite.

I've chosen json over pickle as I prefer it.

The file format is fairly simple each line describes a key/value pair for the module, currently only json types (as with the sqlite version).  Lines can also clear a value.  So we just append changes to the file rather than rewriting it all the time.  When we read the file we just replay the changes.  When we read the file we 'vacuum' it so it only has current values (to keep it small).

I need to check it with python 2/3 unicode etc, add comments, make things more robust eg corrupt files and simultaneous writes from different module threads but the basic idea is here so it'd be good to know if you are happy with the general direction.

 - The driver methods to get the value of a char calls `get_value`
 - `get_value` updates the value internally for the char, it can return the new value

Thanks. I simply tried to make generic what I built for myself.

1) The *signals* functionality cannot be so smart and implement those conditional rules. It's the responsibility of the signals to implement them themselves.

The example should actually not be that difficult. A signal that is `True` if any of the previous `15` bars is `True`.  Some other things may be a bit more elegant, but the thing is about getting the job done ...

```python
class Condition1(bt.Signal):
    def __init__(self)
        realsignal = self.data < bt.indicators.BollingerBands(period=15)
        last15 = bt.indicators.Sum(realsignal, period=15)
        self.lines.signal = last15 > 0.0
```

2/ The `SQN` analyzer is not *timeframe* dependent. It works by counting *trades* (not *orders*) A *trade* is closed when the *size* of a position goes back to `0` or crosses `0`

Hi Juanjo,

The datasource doesn't know in advance how many lines it will have. Instantiating "data" has only created the object and set the parameters that will be used when the data feed fetches the data.

The reasoning behind this: *the data feed can be retrieving data from a live source and therefore ... the number of lines cannot be known in advance*

In any case and because you are only presumably backtesting with your `GenericCSV_TEST` you can know during the execution phase how many lines have been **preloaded** if you tell `cerebro` preload (at instantiation or when invoking `run`)

For example (not tested, it can have some syntax errors):

```python
class MyStrategy(bt.Strategy):
    def next(self):
        print('Total data lines: {}'.format(self.data.buflen()))
        print('Current data lines {}'.format(len(self.data)))

modpath = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(sys.argv[0]))
datapath = ('./datas/IBEALL.csv')

fromdate=datetime.date(2015, 4,1)
todate=datetime.date(2015,12, 31)   

data = bt.feeds.GenericCSV_TEST(
    dataname=datapath, fromdate=fromdate,
    todate=todate,)

cerebro = bt.Cerebro(preload=True)
cerebro.adddata(data)
cerebro.addstrategy(MyStrategy)

cerebro.run()
```

Having a look at the code, the *strategies* are instantiated before the data is *preloaded*. This could possibly (would need to check if it breaks any existing code or it has a side-effect) be turned around, so that the during the `__init__` phase of the strategy, the length of the preloaded data can be queried.

- Mapping functionality is now built into GridMapper classes, which are extensions of the ImageGrid / SubGrid classes but also have functionality to map their 1D results to 2D. These are setup using the usual Mask functions, however instead of using a circular / annulus mask they are completely unmasked (except for the blurring region edges)

- I extended the transform_grid wrapper in profiles such that if the grid that is passed in is an instance of a GridMapper, the result that is returned (e.g. intensitites) is mapped to 2D. If the result is deflection angles, a list of 2D arrays is returned correspond to the x / y values.

- In MaskedImage, I setup a second GridCollection which is a collection of these GridMappers (image, sub and blurring). This ensures we can seamlessly pass our mapper-grids into ray-tracing like we do normal grids.

I am still convinced that the ideal solution binds both to the parent and the root.

> Of course, this same computation happens during verification too which means that if the user cannot be trusted to hold N, P without some "encapsulation" that protects them (it doesn't), the services trusted by the user where N, P will be used as part of a proof should not be trusted either, for they will be able to undo all the encapsulation.

The encapsulation I was referring to is specifically a construction-time property. Macaroons constructed with HMAC of course means that a verifier (root key holder) can build any macaroon with that key that they wish to.

> A poorly implemented discharge service (and I'm betting it's very easy to implement one of these) could be asked to give a discharge N for which the user knows the secret used to construct N. From there, it can look at all of N's caveats and compute the root for P along the way, stripping the encapsulation.

But a properly implemented discharge service could do this correctly? Without binding to the parent it's not even an option.

> It is an anti-pattern to have a discharge service generating third-party caveats and providing a discharge macaroon for those caveats, unless the all macaroons are locally constructed.

I'm not sure it's an anti-pattern outside of what current macaroon implementations can do and are used for. If any implementation supported structural caveats, discharges could become much more interesting ("Expect discharge N, but N must have at least one discharge from (P, Q, R)")

I guess if I needed to sum up my position on this: binding to both the root and the parent is most flexible for users and at least as safe as just binding to the root.

```
""" The  process to be traced """
import os
```     

**signalhunter.py**
```
""" 
Installs a signal handler that execs "/tmp/hunterscript.py" on SIGUSR2.
Sending the signal again toggles the tracing off. 
"""
import signal
import hunter

_hunting = False
def handler(signum, frame):
    """ Toggle hunter tracing """
    global _hunting
    if _hunting:
        _hunting = False
        hunter.stop()
    else:
        _hunting = True
        try:
            script = open("/tmp/hunterscript.py").read()
            exec(script)
        except:
            hunter.stop()
            _hunting = False

signal.signal(signal.SIGUSR2, handler)
```

I'm aware that it's good practice to only install what you use, and I agree with that general principle.

Simply counting CVEs against Kerberos is always going to be skewed because it was released in 1987, 12 years before the CVE database was even created. That's many years of security research. On the other hand, the code base is extremely complicated and probably only a handful of developers know how to implement it correctly.

However, if the objective is to discourage use of Kerberos, then for most Exchange users the alternatives are NTLM and Basic auth. NTLM has over 1.000 hits when I search in CVE (mentions, not CVE numbers, I couldn't find an easy way to do the latter), and is considered insecure and even more awful than Kerberos (newer versions of NTLM is even wrapped in Kerberos, AFAIK). And Basic auth is also considered insecure - for one, it sends passwords clear-text over the wire. Digest auth is rarely availble, at least in my experience.

So, with the given options of authenticating against an Exchange server, I would actually promote the use of Kerberos auth, rather than discourage it by not having it available in the default install. I think it would make more sense security-wise to remove the NTLM package by default, but that would probably inconvenience at least 70% of our current users.

Mind you, we may still want to remove Kerberos as a default package, I just want to make sure we're doing it for the right reasons. Let's keep the discussion open.

The problem she talks about is a real and important problem. Most models are trained on whatever datasets are most easily available (for example, pictures of celebrities who have lots of images online). This means that the models can do worse for people not well represented in the training data. The trainined models will reflect whatever biases exist in the training data. For example, this module tends to have lower accuracy with people from Asia.

The way to fix the problem is to improve the training data to have a wider range of people. But that's surprisingly difficult because getting access to millions of labeled pictures of people's faces is not easy if you aren't Google or Facebook.

Face detection accuracy can be improved with relatively few images, but face recognition requires millions of images to train the model. Just adding in a few new samples won't help anything. You need to add hundreds of thousands of new samples.

As such, I don't know the answer to this problem yet. To make this program better, I wish there was an open source set of face images where people could contribute images for the benefit of everyone. But at the same time, there are obvious problems with building a public dataset like that with personal data.

To answer your specific question, it's not really practical for you to try to retain the face recognition model yourself. To do that, you'd need a few million pictures of faces separated by identity and a relatively beefy GPU. And even if someone else already has a dataset of millions of pictures like that for you to start with, those datasets aren't easy to share for various legal reasons (copyright, personal privacy, etc).

I'm sorry but I fully disagree.

1) Python-Redmine uses relative imports which means that it will always use the bundled Requests library and not the one from the system or from virtualenv or another one installed elsewhere.
2) The size of the package increased by a few hundred kilobytes which is nothing in the current world of terabytes.
3) The license of the bundled package is already included with Python-Redmine (see NOTICE).

Python-Redmine exists for more than 1.5 years now and I had several bug reports connected with the wrong Requests library version on the system, which was very hard to identify. Also, a lot of packages bundle other packages and that is a fact of life, because bundling makes the developer life easier and it also provides users of the package with the best possible experience. For example take the Requests library, it bundles ``chardet`` and ``urllib3`` packages, and ``urllib3`` also bundles ``ordered_dict``, ``six`` and ``ssl_match_hostname`` packages. There was already the same discussion about the Requests library, you can see it [here](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/pull/1812), I agree with everything that was said in there so I'm not going to repeat the same things.

I really appreciate your work for making Python-Redmine available in Debian and Ubuntu, but bundling libraries makes life better, not worse, that is why I'm not going to change my mind about this.

You will be vulnerable to XSS either way. The difference is if the JWT is in the localStorage then an XSS attack will allow the attacker to grab the JWTs, whereas if the JWT is in a `httponly` cookie an XSS attack would allow the attacker to make requests as the user, but not actually steal the JWTs for attacks at a later date. You also have the benefit of setting the `secure` flag on the cookie, so that the browser will never send the cookies (and thus the JWTs) to an endpoint that is not secured with https.

The downside to these things is you are now vulnerable to CSRF attacks. You can avoid the CSRF attacks by utilizing double submit verification. In a nutshell you need to grab a special CSRF token and send it in as an additional header in every request you make to the backend. By default there is a cookie that you can get the key from using javascript. You can also send the CSRF token back in your JSON payload in your login/refresh endpoints, and store it in localStorage to be retrieve via javascript when you make the requests. See these links for specifics

* http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tokens_in_cookies.html
* http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/options.html#cross-site-request-forgery-options

With the issue of `MyModel.objects.get(pk=1)` on models with more than one primary key field. I've added a new model `class Meta` attribute. So that `pk` field must be explicitly set on there if there is more than one primary_key field on a model. E.g.

```python
class CassandraThingMultiplePK(DjangoCassandraModel):
    id = cassandra_columns.UUID(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
    another_id = cassandra_columns.UUID(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
    data_abstract = cassandra_columns.Text(max_length=10)
    created_on = cassandra_columns.DateTime()

    class Meta:
        get_pk_field = 'id'
```

> If the primary goal of email_validator's normalization is to allow foo@gmail.com to still be able to log in if they type foo@GMAIL.COM

I hadn't really thought that far ahead. The goal is really validation, and some normalization in the domain part is required to do validation (of IDNA domains in particular). On top of that, [RFC 6532 (internationalized email) section 3.1](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6532#section-3.1) strongly suggests that the whole email address be NFC-normalized prior to going on the wire.

How particular ISPs map multiple email addresses to the same inbox is a separate concern (though I see where you're coming from).

> But I wonder if adding just a few simple rules for the major providers linked to above (downcasing, +tag stripping, and dot-stripping for gmail) would cover 99% of cases, and so would be worth it?

I think it depends. As far as I'm aware, email addresses _can_ be case sensitive. It's just that no sane ISP allows that. But it would be incorrect to downcase addresses if somewhere for someone that would misroute their email (I think). Similarly for +tag stripping, since presumably not every ISP uses +tag aliases and somewhere `a+1` and `a+2` could be different people.

And for ISPs that do support +tags, it would be wrong to "normalize" an address by removing the +tag. Normalization shouldn't change the email address. The idea of +tags is that they are distinct email addresses (e.g. so that different filters can be applied). Checking if two email addresses are owned by the same person would have to be different from normalization, then.

So I think we're back to the beginning. ISP-specific normalization rules isn't a bad idea, especially downcasing the local part, but I still think it's a losing battle to do it reliably.

If your model is hybridizable, and you want to shelf it for a while, the recommended way would be to use `.export('prefix', epoch=0)`.
That way you can reload it later in a symbol block, or in a different language binding altogether.

If you are iterating with your model, the recommend way is `save_params/load_params` for the parameters, and save the model definition file in a `.py` file or simply pickle it.

re: 10 ms.  Yeah, it may be a little short.  In practice, we just gave up the lock after processing updates.  I think I'll bump this up to 100ms so that it is bounded and short, but not too short. (we can always come back and dispatch on a subsequent go around).  I'll make this configurable in case we later regret this choice.

The idea for this PR is that we sometimes see hh_server queries timeout after 2 minutes with no evidence of why in the logs (it's not sync_timeout) so the implication is that we're stuck somewhere.  The idea here is that we can set an upper bound on the time we're willing to wait for the cases where we can safely fail out early (such as queries) and have that report reasonably back to the client so that we have more context than just a hard socket timeout.

@achimnol using zmq.FD would not be correct. This is not the underlying FD of the socket, but an edge-triggered FD-like API for signaling zmq events (of any kind) on a socket, and quite difficult to use reliably, and one that I wouldn't recommend anyone use unless absolutely necessary. pyzmq doesn't use this for eventloop integration, instead it uses an implementation of the inner loop that uses `zmq_poll`, so that zmq sockets can be monitored directly, rather than through this extra API.

@RojavaCrypto your example isn't working because the right eventloops aren't running:

1. asyncio hasn't been told to use zmq
2. tornado is told to use asyncio, then told again to use zmq instead of asyncio

This patch should get the right stack of things:

1. tell asyncio to use zmq
2. tell tornado to use asyncio

```diff
diff --git a/examples/asyncio/tornado_asyncio.py b/examples/asyncio/tornado_asyncio.py
index 83f562f..3f707b0 100644
--- a/examples/asyncio/tornado_asyncio.py
+++ b/examples/asyncio/tornado_asyncio.py
@@ -1,6 +1,12 @@
 import asyncio
 import zmq.asyncio
+
+from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
 from tornado.platform.asyncio import AsyncIOMainLoop
+
+# tell asyncio to use zmq's eventloop
+zmq.asyncio.install()
+# tell tornado to use asyncio
 AsyncIOMainLoop().install()
 
 # This must be instantiated after the installing the IOLoop
@@ -12,6 +18,7 @@ async def pushing():
     server.bind('tcp://*:9000')
     while True:
         await server.send(b"Hello")
+        await asyncio.sleep(1)
 
 async def pulling():
     client = ctx.socket(zmq.PULL)
@@ -21,7 +28,6 @@ async def pulling():
         print(greeting)
 
 def zmq_tornado_loop():
-    from zmq.eventloop.ioloop import IOLoop
     loop = IOLoop.current()
     loop.spawn_callback(pushing)
     loop.spawn_callback(pulling)
```

> [You don't have to. The warning is just that: a warning. No error occurs when you see it, and it is not an indication of the program doing the wrong thing. You can get Marionette driver support already in version 0.7.4 thru:
- Selenium capabilities for Firefox driver (https://github.com/cobrateam/splinter/pull/417).

Here are the steps that I had to take
- Download the appropriate geckodriver (https://github.com/mozilla/geckodriver/releases)
- Place geckodriver in a location specified by your PATH variable
- Symlink geckodriver to wires `ln -s geckodriver wires`
- Use the following code (change to your firefox binary location):
```python
from splinter import Browser
from selenium.webdriver.common.desired_capabilities import DesiredCapabilities


caps = DesiredCapabilities.FIREFOX
caps["marionette"] = True
caps["binary"] = "/Applications/Firefox.app/Contents/MacOS/firefox-bin"

browser = Browser('firefox', capabilities=caps)
```

Code derived from: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/QA/Marionette/WebDriver

```
vms = get_obj(content, [vim.VirtualMachine], None)

   vm.Unregister()
```

I still think this is a good idea, but I think the tooling landscape has changed sufficiently to make it worthwhile to reassess whether an in-place rewrite is still preferable to the alternative option of introducing a second venv management library:

* Option 1: make the rewrite a direct successor to the existing `virtualenv`, as this PR does, such that all `virtualenv` usage is implicitly upgraded to the new approach (with the associated backwards compatibility risks) unless folks explicitly restrict themselves to an older version of `virtualenv`
* Options2: make the rewrite a distinct project, but also update higher level integration projects (`tox`, `vex`, `pew`, `pipenv`, `pyenv`, `hatch`, etc) to switch to depending on the new library, rather than the traditional one

The UX difference between the two approaches is essentially as follows:

* For option 1, `virtualenv < 16` would essentially become the name of "traditional virtualenv". If anyone wanted to release bug fixes for that (or make it compatible with new Python versions), then they'd need to do a new 15.X.Y release from the main virtualenv repo
* For option 2, the default would be for people to stay on the old implementation, *unless* they made the conscious decision to switch to the new one (or else were using a higher level integration project that made the switch on their behalf)

Putting it like that, I think it's clear that despite the potential compatibility risks, the in-place rewrite is likely still the better approach, as it offers the clearest long-term path to switching entirely to PEP 405 based virtual environments as various other projects are able to start dropping support for Python 2.7.

The backwards compatibility risks could then be mitigated by:

1. Providing notice (perhaps even via a `virtualenv 15.1.1` release) that the implementation strategy will be changing in the next major version
2. Publishing a beta virtualenv rewrite that passes its own test suite
3. Getting some of the key integration projects (as listed above) to run their own tests against the beta version (or even the git branch *before* `virtualenv` publishes the beta release)
4. Iterate until the `virtualenv` devs are happy that the remaining compatibility issues are going to be relatively esoteric ones
5. Actually make the release

Ok, on further investigation this is not a regression.

1. The URL points to a `.gz` file, and currently we are only able to determine if file is an archive based on its extension. Thus, any content retrieved from a URL is assumed to be a plain text.
2. When parsing a binary file, in assertion `firstJumpEnd && topQuoteRule >= 0` both conditions fail. Thus, "topQuoteRule" logic is not the culprit here: the assert was failing before too.
3. The reason the assert fails is because `ctx.countfields()` returns `-1` for every combination of `sep/quoteRule`. This happens because of NUL bytes: a string parser stumbles upon those and cannot parse.

Thus, this issue is directly related to #790.

Another problem is what happens in production, when `xassert`s are turned off? We proceed with parsing the file while having `firstJumpEnd=NULL` and `topQuoteRule=-1`, which won't work well...

I think Font Awesome might just start using [unicode-range](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/%40font-face/unicode-range) to resolve this issue entirely and leave everyone happy :smiley:

It is [well supported](http://caniuse.com/#search=unicode-range) nowadays, so having icons split by categories or in some other way into blocks will allow to only have small overhead of loading few more icons from the same block, but not loading the whole font at once.

CSS can be also split into corresponding categories with own `@font-face {..unicode-range:  ..}` and definitions of icons from that block. This way everyone will get what he/she wants and still there will be no fundamental separation of Font Awesome into several separate projects.

There will be multiple css+fonts files combinations, each for separate category/block and some master CSS that imports them all together (many people will use build systems to inline imports anyway + growing adoption of HTTP2 makes overhead even smaller, so this is not a major issue I think).

These are native features available within web platform today, why not use them?

@tagliala, @davegandy, what do you think about such approach?

The way it works underneath (to me it was important to confirm this):

1. The file object is passed as `data` to the `copyfrom` method
1. The `CopySQLClient` uses the `APIKeyAuthClient`,  that in turn uses the `BaseAuthClient` from `pyrestcli`
1. The call to `self.client.send` is [actually calling `self.session.request`](https://github.com/danicarrion/pyrestcli/blob/0f505b2f2cb4d9ffd80fa8dd3cc77ea0eb41b6fc/pyrestcli/auth.py#L36) which uses the `requests` library
1. That calls `requests.Session.requests` which calls to `requests.Session.send` which [delegates the call to an adapter](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/e4d214dd763caf9a59dba0b382106cbf9938aa51/requests/sessions.py#L625), particularly the `HTTPAdapter`
1. The data is transferred in chunks [here](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/e4d214dd763caf9a59dba0b382106cbf9938aa51/requests/adapters.py#L465)


As you can see, the only requirement for the `data` is to be an interator of some sort. There's also a test for it [here](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/d2e1130603c67d278eb49980205a3797e9e8b4a3/tests/test_lowlevel.py#L16). The `request.body` is populated [here](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/d2e1130603c67d278eb49980205a3797e9e8b4a3/requests/models.py#L472-L473) BTW.

> I looked at RFC-7089 and it does not require a memento (or last memento) relation in the Link header for Pattern 1.1 or 2.1, so sites may not consistently list this relation, requiring one to make a request of the URI-M for the Memento-Datetime. 1. perform the request on the URI-G with the Accept-Datetime
2. get the URI-M from the Location header *or Content-Location header* in the response
3. parse the link header using `parse_link_header` to get the Memento-Datetime from the memento relation or the memento last relation, depending on whether Accept-Datetime was submitted *or use actual Memento-Datetime header, if present*
4. use `__prepare_memento_response`, passing it the URI-M, the Memento-Datetime, and the Link header to produce the JSON response

Hi Manuel, 
   You're correct that there is a mutex used, so the claim of being lockless isn't 100% accurate. Nevertheless, the forward and backward passes, which account for the overwhelming majority of computation do not use locks internally.

   The misunderstanding here seems to be what the call to update() does. Gradients are computed by the backward() function, and the gradients are stored in the Parameter objects. The update() call simply loops over all parameters and does the SGD (or Adam, etc.) update on each parameter. This means the lock is only acquired once ever N examples, and is only held for a brief amount of time.

  As for which parts of the code are done by the parent, the parent is the process with cid of 0. The parent also loops over examples and computes gradients. The only difference is that when the parent finishes computing the gradients for an example it calls update() and outputs the loss info to stderr.

  The averaging of gradients is done in two steps. First, note that as backward() computes the gradients for each parameter it sums the new gradients with whatever gradients were already stored in the Parameter object. This means that if you call backward() N times, the gradients stored in the Parameter objects are the sum of the gradients of N examples. Then when we call update, we pass in a scaling factor of 1.0 / counter, where counter counts the number of examples seen since the last update. This has the effect of dividing the gradients by counter, giving us a true average.

Hi @jashugan 

As a basic sanity check I verified that this unicode string in the corrected form `u'Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272'` correctly roundtrips to other languages and within the Python kit itself (through manual additions to regression round trips and the [bin/verify corner-case suite](https://github.com/cognitect/transit-format/blob/master/src/transit/corner_cases.clj) in [transit-format](https://github.com/cognitect/transit-format).

The behavior you're observing is related to an additional `ensure_ascii` step that `json.dumps` includes. If you switch this behavior off, you get the same output as transit's writer (for the non-error producing cases):

```
In [13]: json.dumps(u'Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272' , ensure_ascii=False)
Out[13]: u'"Av. Za\xf1artu 1482, \xd1u\xf1oa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272"'
```
This matches Python's literal behavior and produces human readable unicode:

```
In [17]: u'Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272'
Out[17]: u'Av. Za\xf1artu 1482, \xd1u\xf1oa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272'
```

```
In [18]: print(u'Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272')
Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272
In [19]: print(json.dumps(u'Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272'))
"Av. Za\u00f1artu 1482, \u00d1u\u00f1oa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272"
```

This is expected behavior due to how USB DAQ devices operate.  To achieve maximum throughput, samples are transferred from the USB device to the host device (your computer) in chunks.  Calling available_samp_per_chan only indicates the number of samples already available on your host machine.  In other words it is the number of samples that are immediately available if you call read.

When you specify a specific number of samples when calling a read the driver to send a message to the USB device telling it to immediately flush the requested samples to the host making them available.  The function blocks until the samples are transferred.  When you ask for only available samples, the read will return immediately with 0 samples initially but if continued will eventually return samples.  The driver sends a message to the USB device to start flushing its buffer when you execute the first read available samples if no samples are currently available.

If recent fleet should be used, then it should be either downloaded on each boot or stored in some persistent storage (probably somewhere in `/var` as `/usr` is readonly in CoreOS).

I would love to be able to do this, but I'm pretty sure that any naive implementation of this would be inherently unsafe.

Updating the contents of the virtualenv under which the current console script is running is inherently dangerous and not something we really should want to play with.
Plenty of things won't break, but `pkg_resources` could easily break down catastrophically, and we never know when we use library functions how much they're relying on their disk state.
Even if it works today, there is no guarantee that it will work on any given subsequent update.

If we want this, we need to think of a way of doing this that successfully isolates the running update from the invoking python command. I'm unable to think of one at present, but perhaps we'll come up with something.

``` Python

import pandas_datareader.data as web
import datetime
import matplotlib.pylab as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

start = datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 1)
end = datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 27)
f = web.DataReader("F", 'google', start, end)

def plot_x_and_rfft(x):

    fft = np.fft.rfft(x.values)
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))
    
    plt.subplot(311)
    plt.title("Time Series")
    plt.plot(x)
    
    plt.subplot(312)
    plt.plot(fft.real, label="real", marker="o")
    plt.plot(fft.imag, label="imag", marker="o")
    plt.title("Real and imaginary part of DFT coefficients A(k)")
    plt.legend()

    plt.subplot(313)
    plt.plot(fft.real[1:], label="real", marker="o")
    plt.plot(fft.imag[1:], label="imag", marker="o")
    plt.title("Real and imaginary part of DFT coefficients A(k), without first")
    plt.legend()
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_x_and_rfft(f["High"])
```

I think cockroachdb/cockroach#16846 solves the main problem here, which is that after certain errors the `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT` would reliably fail.

There's a secondary issue that if `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT` fails for other reasons (e.g. network failure), its error clobbers the underlying error that caused the rollback. I don't think we need to do anything special here - Python 3's exception chaining already provides all the errors, so nothing is completely hidden, and there's no special handling that is necessary when these network errors occur (unlike with the original error here, in which the ConditionFailedError might be handled specially).

Which version of Curator did you install?  The command line you're attempting to use is from Curator 1.x., but `from .cli import cli` indicates a much more recent version.

Also, how did you install Curator?  You're attempting to launch `curator.py`, which is not how it's supposed to be run.  Curator installs an `entry_point` at `/usr/local/bin/curator` or on some systems, `/usr/bin/curator`.  You will always see the `relative import` message if you try to execute `curator.py` from the module path, rather than using the endpoint.

You should try just running: `curator --help` from the command line and see what you get.

@jjcollinge thank you for reporting this issue. The reason why your sas token doesn't work is that you created a __table__ sas token, which manages the permission of access objects in the scope of given table. To generate a sas token to validate the existence of a table, you need a sas token at the account level.

Here's an example:
``` BASH
10:35 $ az storage account generate-sas --expiry '2017-12-31T00:00Z' --permissions ldpruwac --resource-types sco --services bfqt
"se=2017-12-31T00%3A00Z&sp=rwdlacup&sv=2016-05-31&ss=bqtf&srt=sco&sig=TzTpRlOK/kTIf13A5T5byool2S3/8LOMtJ%2BSPH5qJqQ%3D"

10:35 $ az storage table exists -n table01 --account-name trdaitest01 --sas-token 'se=2017-12-31T00%3A00Z&sp=rwdlacup&sv=2016-05-31&ss=bqtf&srt=sco&sig=TzTpRlOK/kTIf13A5T5byool2S3/8LOMtJ%2BSPH5qJqQ%3D'
{
  "exists": true
}
```
_In above command, I grand all permissions on all types of resources. You can tailor the scope based on your need._

Anyways, according to the sounddevice library that is actually returning the underflow indicator, the underflow bool is set to true when a gap is inserted into output data, possibly because the stream callback is using too much CPU time
https://github.com/spatialaudio/python-sounddevice/blob/master/sounddevice.py#L1783-L1793

That file holds too many layers of abstraction for audio I/O for it's own good, but that's okay. In the snippet I linked, they say that the underflow might happen during "play()". Turns out, play() calls upon an OutputStream object which again is an abstraction of the RawOutputStream object which is ultimately what is interfacing with the audiosetting the underflow flag. The Google Assistant example is skipping a bunch of the abstractions and writes the buffer straight to RawOutputStream.write() where the underflow error is being tripped due to what seems to be skipped samples due to performance issues. I'm not sure how much deeper I can dig, the next layer of abstraction is a CFFI and that's a whole other can of beans, but it points to a bunch of different audio backends depending on the platform it's running on
That was a bit of a mess but basically the Google sample skips some abstraction in the sounddevice library and writes a buffer straight to a RawOutputStream, which works via a C Foreign Function Interface down to your Pi's audio framework which is ALSA with what I think is a JACK server on top. At this point we're at the Linux kernel so I don't think changing things here is the way to go to say the least.
I would try to change the settings in the Google example to be more performance oriented, like lowering the sample rate/width. I'm not sure if the block size should be bigger or smaller, but play around with each of the settings individually until you find something you think is an acceptable compromise between sound quality and performance

According to the troubleshooting part in the readme.md of the google assistant sdk repo, stuttering audio may be fixed by setting the block size to a value larger than the size of the "ConverseResponse" audio payload. I just pushed a branch on top of yours with a few tests: https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/commits/bzr_tests. These are pretty much copies of the hg tests. One of them is failing -- the reason why is that it looks like the bzr plugin never `bzr pull` when `rev` isn't defined. That means that for example `peru sync --no-cache` will fail to actually re-fetch a bzr repo. We should probably have `pull_if_needed` explicitly check whether a `rev` was provided, and if not always pull.

Once that's done, I think we're good to go.

Another difference that came up when I was writing the reup test was that I noticed the bzr plugin doesn't support the (somewhat confusingly named) `reup` field that the git and hg plugins support. This lets you specify what branch the plugin should look to determine the latest rev, when running `peru reup`. If you want, we could add that in, or we can just get back to it later if anyone needs it.

After I fixed that, the public IP was assigned but the public DNS hostname wasn't. The custom VPC needs to be changed to fix that:

Lastly, there also needs to be at least one NAT gateway in the VPC, ideally one per availability zone. Without it, the instance won't be able to reach the outside world and cgcloud will hang waiting for SSH to be functional.

coming back on my earlier comments, I think `select_for_update` is not necessary when doing an atomic `update`, you would only use it where you are first selecting, then modifying the instance, then saving

you could manually send those signals for the affected rows, e.g.

```python
from django.db import router
from django.db.models import signals

def allocate(self, quantity):
    """
    Record a stock allocation.

    This normally happens when a product is bought at checkout.  When the
    product is actually shipped, then we 'consume' the allocation.
    """

    self.__class__.objects.filter(pk=self.pk).update(
        num_allocated=Case(
            When(num_allocated__isnull=True,
                 then=(0 + quantity)),
            default=F('num_allocated') + quantity
        ),
    )
    signals.post_save.send(
        sender=self.__class__,
        instance=self,
        created=False,
        raw=False,
        using=router.db_for_write(self.__class__, instance=self)
    )
allocate.alters_data = True
```

* Yes, the ICloneable interface can be removed - it's not used by the .NET Core Framework. If you need cloning functionality, you can still keep the methods you've implemented (or provide your own ICloneable interface if it's useful to your apps).
* ManualResetEvent.Close can be replaced with ManualResetEvent.Dispose.
* For Attribute.IsDefined, use the System.Reflection.CustomAttributeExtensions.IsDefined() extension method the in System.Reflection.Extensions contract.
* I'm not very familiar with JsonFormatter, but it's probably possible to get similar functionality with the Newtonsoft.Json package or with the DataContractSerializer type.
* Configuration has changed in .NET Core since different targets will have different underling config systems. If this is for ASP.NET, you can use the new [ASP.NET configuration system](https://docs.asp.net/en/latest/fundamentals/configuration.html).

I rewrote the tests for the simplified ineichen function and improved its nan handling. A couple of API things to consider:

1. Should the linke_turbidity, dni_extra, and altitude parameters should have default values? The simplfied_solis function has default values for pw, aod, dni_extra, and pressure. It's probably best if they have similar default values.
2. Should the ineichen (and simplified_solis) models accept solar zenith or solar elevation as an input? The ineichen function was originally written in terms of zenith, so that's what I used as a function argument. Later, I reread the Ineichen and Perez paper and discovered that the paper uses elevation, not zenith. The simplified solis paper and function is written in terms of elevation. Again, probably best if they're consistent.
3. Ineichen is behind both the "ineichen" and the simplified solis models. Maybe there's a better name for the ineichen function... ineichenperez, ineichen_turbidity, ineichen_perez_linke_turbidity, kip (kasten, ineichen, perez). I'm ok leaving it alone, too.

@arranf 
> Can I ask why you have the negations in your version above?

Those aren't negations. This is SMT-LIB's syntax for putting an attribute on expression.  E.g.

```
(! (<some expression) : named foo)
```

In the example I gave using the unsat core I'm giving each expression given to `(assert )`  the `:named` attribute (e.g. `a0`, `a1`). This is needed when you request the unsat core because when the unsat core is reported it is given in terms of the named attributes.

The question about the scope of Lasagne is an interesting one.  Is it a 
tool for Theano users that want to build things from scratch?  Probably 
even seasoned Theano users could benefit from things like training loop 
code that allows them to write less code and concentrate on their own 
problem faster.

The question remains whether Lasagne also wants to be attractive to 
users that *aren't* familiar with Theano.  I get the feeling that a lot 
of nolearn.lasagne users have little idea about how to use Theano but 
they can still use the tools to good effect.  And it doesn't take a lot 
of code to allow them this more gentle intro to Lasagne.

I agree with Sander, for backwards compatibility we cannot easily change the defaults. If you train very deep networks and need to be careful about the scale, note that data-dependent initialization is not very difficult to implement: http://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06422
This may even allow you to limit batch normalization to correcting the mean only: http://arxiv.org/abs/1602.07868

> We still need a way to allow custom output shapes. For example, blocks defines two additional parameters unused_edge and original_image_size for this: https://github.com/mila-udem/blocks/blob/b853b570f14e7494c5f95179f1cd977ffa69e91b/blocks/bricks/conv.py#L189-L204
The first one gives the number of additional zeros to pad the right and bottom border of the input with, allowing values between 0 and stride - 1. The second one directly gives the output size (a bit more difficult to check for correctness, but probably easier for the user). Which one(s) should we support?

I agree that the second one is probably easier for the user. The typical use case I would expect is an shrinking encoding pathway followed by one or more `TransposeConv2DLayer` to restore the original resolution. To specify the `unused_edge` the user would have to do some (possibly nasty) trickery to detect the amount of *implicit cropping* done by the encoding layers (e.g., a valid convolution 2x2 will implicitly crop one column/row if the input is odd, but will not crop anything on an even input). Specifying the desired shape should instead be easy.

> Both won't give a convenient way of inverting a convolution with variable input size, as only a MergeLayer can query the symbolic input shape of a convolution to be inverted. Probably we should leave that job for the InverseLayer, possibly along with #447.

I didn't look into your code yet, but I guess both arguments could be allowed to be symbolical values (meaning accept either symbolical or numerical values). If that's possible, then the user can just find his own way to get a tensor with the desired (variable) padding or shape. That would be ideal.

About current libc function abstraction, It seems that angr has only too modes:
1. use the libc func abstraction model
2. not using it at all

I think a third model maybe worth doing @zardus @rhelmot @ltfish :
concrete exec it when input data are concrete, use the abstraction only when symbols are involved.
simulating library codes would be tedious and error-prone with various corner cases and thus decrease scalability to real-world software. on the other side, due to path explosion, without abstraction, when symbolic data are involved, analysis may not be effective.
this mode perhaps can be implemented lazily, just concrete exec library function first, when symbolic values encountered, then backtrace to the function entry and using the abstraction to do the real work.

There's one thing that would be good to add (at least in the announcement email, not sure about in the release notes themselves): a description of what PyWavelets is. For numpy/scipy I never bothered because everyone knows what those are, but for v0.3.0 someone sent me an email suggesting "next time, tell me what this PyWavelets thing is".

I am not opposed to updating to numpy 1.8, but that should probably be a separate PR.  Your `full` function above is a reasonable alternative too.

As far as applications, it is easier to pass a single array to a function such as `pywt.threshold` than build a uitility function to loop over a list of arrays and coefficient dictionaries.  Also, wavelets are a popular choice of regularizer in iterative image reconstruction algorithms.  Such algorithms often write the DWT as a linear operator that acts on a vector of image pixels and returns a vector of wavelet coefficients, so if you want to interface with a third-party algorithm, it is easier to have the output in this common format.  Within the algorithms, thresholding of the coefficients and computations of norms of the coefficients are common.

As far as`return_array` is concerned, I think it is a question of whether the convenience is worth the additional API complication.  I don't have too strong of a preference either way.  I agree that if we do go the `return_array` route, we would need to support it as an input format for `waverecn`, etc.

In terms of computation time, the coefficient array conversion seems to be an order of magnitude faster than the transform itself for 2D or 3D inputs, but only modestly faster in the 1D case.  That seems reasonable enough to me given that it is a pure python implementation:

*** on my PC for Python 3.4***:
3D shape (256x256x256):  `wavedecn` takes 2.5 s, and the conversion 87 ms.
2D shape (256x256): `wavedecn` takes 5.06 ms and the conversion takes 0.28 ms.
1D shape (256,):  `wavedecn` takes 0.21 ms and the conversion takes 0.14 ms.

If you are using the D415, D435 or the SR300 device, the color camera is physically displaced relative to the "depth" camera. This means that pixel {x,y} in color stream will not correspond to {x,y} in the depth stream. Assuming what you want is a single RGBD image, you must find the correspondence between every two pixels and "align" the two streams. 
Finding the correspondence is easy assuming one of the streams is depth - you can project 2D pixel in to 3D world using depth and then project it back to 2D pixel relative to the second camera coordinate system. 
Whatever stream you chose to align to, the post condition of `align` is that all output frames will share that target stream resolution.

Depth generated from stereo can often suffer from this kind of noise. 
It really depends on:
1. Camera calibration (we have previously provided dynamic re-calibration application, and I believe such application is also available for the D400 series, but we will have to look-up the link)
2. Camera preset - if you are interested in depth accuracy you can prioritize it over fill rate using `High Accuracy` preset. Presets are available in the Viewer and also from code.
3. Post-processing - in recent versions of the SDK we offer several post-processing algorithms that reduce depth noise. You can enable and configure them individually in the Viewer or programmatically (we are about to publish an example that shows how to do this from code) 
4. The scene. You can still get depth from the D400 series even in very bad visual conditions (when laser has to be off, and the IR image is either over or under saturated), but you will get more noise. 

Based on the snapshots, I would recommend to first check presets and post-processing. 

We also provide Depth Quality app in this SDK that let's you play around with presets and see how different settings influence various metrics.

Some comments:
- The way the featurizer is intended to work with most models, probably for most cases you want the "bad bond value" to just be zero, not NaN. i.e., there is no Li-Li bond component to Na2O. It doesn't matter that it's not possible to have that component.
- If you define the element order in the bond in order of Mendeelev number (lowest element Mendeleev number first, highest Mendeelev number second), I think you can have a consistent bond label (no "Li-O" vs "O-Li") *plus* not need to check multiple combinations for distance (just take the direct distance).

A more general comment is that multiple of these featurizers require knowing in advance all the entries of the dataframe in order to determine the feature columns. It might be better to look at the ``fit_transform`` methods in scikit-learn often used for text mining as an inspiration rather than trying to cram things into the current Featurizer format. I'll submit a separate issue about this, just mentioning it here.

I was referring to https://github.com/pypa/packaging/blob/master/docs/specifiers.rst

Basically translating  the commits to vulnerable releases, something like:
```

- name: "Issue #26556: Expat 2.1.1"
  fixed-in:
    - d244a8f7cb0ec6979ec9fc7acd39e95f5339ad0e # 2.7.12
    - 196d7db3956f4c0b03e87b570771b3460a61bab5 # 3.4.5
  description: ...
  vulnerable:
    - >=3.4,<3.4.5
    - >=2.7,<2.7.12
```

This allows to do find vulnerable releases using the `packaging` library:
```
>>> from packaging.specifiers import SpecifierSet
>>> vuln = SpecifierSet(">=3.4,<3.4.5")
>>> vuln.contains("3.4.2")
True
```

@thcu-gp, thanks for raising these issues.  Responses below:

> 1. 'all channels' in OpenThread is specified by `0xffffffff`, which well exceeds the allowed channels in MAC 802154. 

Strictly speaking, `0xffffffff` well exceeds the channels for the **2.4 GHz band** in IEEE 802.15.4-2006.  Thread has provisions for supporting other IEEE 802.15.4 PHYs (i.e. Channel Pages) which may have different channel ranges.  Rather than having separate "all channels" define for each Channel Page, we simplify on one.

> 2. the 'all channels' mask from the MAC 802154 spec uses `0x7fff800` to specify channels 11 to 26. To only uses the channels 11 to 26 in OpenThread, one has to specify `0x0000ffff` as a mask (unintuitive)

The `OT_CHANNEL_*_MASK` [definitions](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/types.h#L383) are consistent with `0x7fff800` to specify channels 11 through 26.

That said, there is a bug in `MeshForwarder` that interprets the channel mask incorrectly.  Will submit a PR soon to fix this.

> 3. If one specifies e.g. the channelmask `0x000f0000` (channels 27 to 31). The check in [mesh_forwarder.cpp](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/thread/mesh_forwarder.cpp) on line 1588, is only triggered after the radio tried to send packets on channel 27 (a check is missing)

Agree that this is an issue.  Will submit a PR to address this issue.

I've got some time to prototype changing the PHP driver's encoding/compatibility features to use static data files in the next few days. I'll be working in [mike42/escpos-snippets](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-snippets). If I can drop the PHP code, I'd be glad to start storing printer compatibility info from a repo shared by open source receipt printing projects, to minimize duplication of effort.

I've had a bit of a think about the data layout, and what I've come up with looks slightly different to this pull request, but with the same features. I think that if we store the data like this, it gives us more usage scenarios. Eg, we could use the data for escpos-php, python-escpos, a parser, render to a web-page, or whatever else.

Can you let me know if you see any issues if I generated YAML files in the format below as a part of this?

The three YAML files model:

- Profiles
- CodePages
- Commands

## Profile

Needs to capture all of the data that you get from a test print on a given printer. Included some free-text fields so that we don't need to comment the file, and a printer list so that [printer lists](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php#printers) can include the best known profile to use.

```yaml
---
default:
  name: Default profile
  description: Default ESC/POS profile for Epson-branded printers
  printers:
  - FooPrint 90000
  - SuperPrint 80
  command:
    LineFeedCmd: false
    FeedAndCutCmd: true
    SetAbsolutePrintPosCmd: true
    GraphicsDataCmd: true
  media:
    width:
      pixels: 768
      mm: 80
  font:
    0:
      columns: 40
    1:
      columns: 50
  codepage:
    0: CP437
    7: TCVN-3-1
  colors: 2
  features:
    starCommands: false

simple:
  name: Simple profile
  inherit: default
  description: Simple profile for printers which do not support more advanced features
  command:
    GraphicsDataCmd: false
...
```

## CodePage

The upper half of the code page (chars 128-255). Encodings are sourced from `iconv`, `custom`, or `none` (stuff we know exists but we can't encode).

I'm suggesting that we pull custom code pages out of the profiles and put them here, as it's really just another data source.

My ideal `codepage.yml` would look like:
```yaml
---
codepage:
  CP437:
    name: OEM font
    provider: iconv
  CP1098:
    name: CP1098 Farsi
    provider: none
  TCVN-3-1:
    name: TCVN-3 Vietnamese 1
    provider: custom
    data:
      - "                "
      - "                "
      - "        ăâêôơưđ "
      - "     àảãáạ ằẳẵắ "
      - "      ặầẩẫấậè ẻẽ"
      - "éẹềểễếệìỉ   ĩíịò"
      - " ỏõóọồổỗốộờởỡớợù"
      - " ủũúụừửữứựỳỷỹýỵ "
...
```

## Command

I've got a parser in the works which has to identify ESC/POS commands, so I have given them all unique names.

Including the command-list will let us query support for a given command, to perform substitutions or disable functionality based on the printer that is connected.

```yaml
---
command:
  LineFeedCmd:
    name: Line feed
  FeedAndCutCmd:
    name: Feed and cut
  SetAbsolutePrintPosCmd:
    name: Set absolute print position
  GraphicsDataCmd:
    name: Graphics data
...
```

> That wouldn't be too hard to pull off for a solitary type like std::vector<int>, but inheritance makes things much tougher. One module would have no information about the type hierarchy of the py::module_local types of another module. One possibility would be to smuggle this type information between modules by attaching it as a capsule to the Python type object (all Python types have a __dict__).

Yes, inheritance should be harder, but that is ok in many cases.  Since a class publicly inheriting from std::vector et al is making an error, classes using std containers should be expected to do so via private inheritance, and thus the buffer protocol and opaque stuff will be more "manual" for those types.  

Except for Eigen/Armadillo types, I'm guessing it'll be fairly rare for two different modules to wrap the same underlying non-std C++ types.  

My specific case where I ran into issues was in developing two different python packages based on two different C++ packages or genetics research.  They are designed so that data can be shuttled to/from via some simple std containers, and it also makes sense for each Python package to provide opaque access w/buffer protocols available for those container types.

I have completely traced that bug
It is in pyopenssl wrapper which is from urllib3's contib (which is used by requests=1.2.3)

There is a special behavior with python OpenSSL and blocking socket during ssl handshakes (or bug, idk)
Urllib3 treats this as handshake error (which is actually not)
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26540429/python-openssl-connection-failing-handshake-when-using-timeout

Error seems to be fixed in urllib3 >=1.8 (have not tested, but since that version they ugly catch that error)

#### Original piece of code on server

```
cnx = OpenSSL.SSL.Connection(ctx, sock)
    cnx.set_tlsext_host_name(server_hostname)
    cnx.set_connect_state()
    try:
        cnx.do_handshake()
    except OpenSSL.SSL.Error as e:
        raise ssl.SSLError('bad handshake', e)

    return WrappedSocket(cnx, sock)
```

#### Starting from version 1.8
```
while True:
        try:
            cnx.do_handshake()
        except OpenSSL.SSL.WantReadError:
            select.select([sock], [], [])
            continue
        except OpenSSL.SSL.Error as e:
            raise ssl.SSLError('bad handshake', e)
        break
```

closing this since I changed approach.

Instead of adding state changes for clearing up failed transactions, which would require the proxies to have code to detect this and know when something is recoverable or not, the business logic was added into the state machines and the existing state changes are used to identify races. The nice thing about the new approach is that less state changes are required.

Note that anything that is not invalidated because of a race which does the same thing or invalidate a transition is considered a bug, and therefor the queue is not automatically cleared.
Implementing these checks at the moment is a bit overkill, the REST API should just use the `async_result` and return a corresponding HTTP RESULT CODE if the transfer succeed or fails.

Not that I know of. I have seen some papers that compare TPOT and auto-sklearn to other algorithms on a handful of datasets and most of the time they come out even, which has been my experience too. IMO the search algorithm matters much less than the pipeline components and the AutoML tool's ability to represent complex pipelines.

I think there has been more focus on developing the offerings of the individual algorithms rather than comparing existing algorithms. For anyone who is interested, I encourage them to pursue this project.

The requests wrt adding a more detailed user guide for TPOT are fair. @ran88dom99, can you please file a separate issue for improving the user guide? I can't guarantee that it will be a top priority, but let's at least get an issue filed for it.

To answer your specific questions:

>Should I set population_size always less than offspring_size?

In general, you want `population_size` to be at least 100, preferably 500+ if you have the time. It's likely not worth changing `offspring_size`. For my 'long' runs, I will often set `population_size` to 1000 and `generations` to 1000. You can also make use of the `early_stopping` parameter to end the TPOT run early if no improvements are being made.

>What to do with small (130) datasets?

Overfitting is a major concern here. I suggest using the 'TPOT light' configuration, as 130 records is likely not enough for the more complex methods used in the default TPOT configuration. Make sure to have a good test set to verify that TPOT is not overfitting on your training set.

>Running TPOT for a day with the right parameters gets cv RMSE from 1 to almost 0 but .44 RMSE on a holdout set. Anything more I can do?

The best solution is to collect more samples. Barring that, you can try the LeaveOneOut `cv` scheme and use the 'TPOT light' configuration if you suspect overfitting is an issue.

Some initial comments before the weekend:
1. The open..() method should not return the entire response body, but instead just the topic string. (This is just for consistency, we generally try to shield the user from request bodies, response bodies, and URIs).
2. This is really quite low level, asking the user to set up JMS communication themselves. With the `MessageChannel` class I described in issue #148, the user could get close support and would be shielded from the JMS protocol.

```python
from ipywidgets import Layout, Button, Box, VBox

items = [Button(description='example') ]
box1 = Box(children=items)
box2 = Box(children=items)

display(VBox([box1, box2, box3]))
```

> Now this is pretty ugly and will make asyncio code slower under PyPy ;) Frankly, I don't think we can implement any revision of the__acall__ idea at this point.

To elaborate: any change to PEP 492 semantics will have to be fully backwards compatible for asyncio programs. Any significant drop in performance wouldn't be acceptable either.

What you are proposing is to make parenthesis a part of the `await` expression. For that you'll need new opcodes. To make your proposal backwards compatible, those new opcodes could execute different versions of async protocol depending on a special flag in the interpreter state. This could really work for code like `await a()`, or even `await a.b()`.  But what about `await a().b()`? You'll have to use `CALL_FUNCTION` at some point, or you will have to reinvent all `CALL_*` opcodes.

Maybe it's possible to come up with some clever idea here, but the patch (and the PEP) will be bigger than the one for PEP 492.

> I guess one approach would be to have the capture code only skip loops, but not throw away "horizontal" duplicates. (Something like, when we recurse we pass through a seen set, but we make a separate copy of it for each entry in a MultiError.)

Just putting down some thoughts to make sure I understand what each part of the multierror actually represents.

**MultiError traceback**: This is the stack from where the MultiError was created to where it was handled.

**MultiError context or cause**: This is any exception that was being handled when the MultiError was created (like if there was a nursery in an except block).

By using the stdlib traceback formatting code, this will work as desired and there should be no duplicates that need to be explicitly printed.

**MultiError sub-exceptions**: Each want to be printed separately. If there are duplicates, especially in different parts of the "tree", we probably want to mention that instead of silently omitting them.

This matches with what you said about "horizontal" duplicates and using a copy of our `_seen` at that point for each. I say a copy rather than an empty seen on the off chance that somehow the context of the MultiError we are handling appears as a cause or context of the sub exception, in which case it would be omitted.

In this way, all sub-exceptions will be printed with a reasonable stack of causes, and there may be duplicates in those stacks. In order to re-use the existing `TracebackException::format` code, we won't be able to mention that the duplicates are duplicates, but as long as the stacks are there it should be okay.

I've been thinking about the best way to implement the scenario you describe. I have a "generic image uploads" (non-ajax) library I use in several projects that does use GFKs, similar to how you've implemented it, and it's worked great for years.  So for those local-storage scenarios, it's a reasonable solution.

My concern is for the other backends.

I'd propose this:

- Make the UploadedFile class a mixin (`UploadedFileMixin`), and store it in the `local_database.py`
- For the models file, check against a django.conf setting, say `AJAX_UPLOADER_MODEL_BACKEND`. 

 - If it's equal to "local_database", then create the model, using the mixin.
 - If it's unset, or has another value, do nothing.
